category_summary:
The **OpenAI-Microsoft** partnership restructuring dominated the day. **Sam Altman** [announced OpenAI can now](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-d17d3c52077d) offer products across all clouds while **Microsoft** remains primary partner. **Simon Willison** [highlighted the removal](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-2ea3a8428abf) of the AGI clause from revenue-sharing terms. Altman also [celebrated strong developer reception](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-37542ee37731) to **GPT-5.5**.

- **China** [blocked **Meta's** $2B acquisition](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-acb3751faf45) of **Manus**, signaling Beijing now treats AI software and talent as strategic national assets ahead of the Trump-Xi meeting
- **Andrew Ng** [published a detailed essay](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-292c33c8e67f) on AI-native software teams — engineers becoming generalists, 1:1 engineer-to-PM ratios, and new downstream bottlenecks
- **hardmaru** [presented an **ICLR 2026** paper](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-a9fed8b7988d) on a Conductor model trained via RL to orchestrate pools of LLMs, powering **Sakana AI's** Fugu system
- **vLLM** [announced day-0 support](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-1e7ac2a3c39b) for **DeepSeek V4** base models, continuing rapid open-source infrastructure buildout
- **Yudkowsky** [made a substantive case](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-0a8b007264cd) for international AI governance treaties, drawing nuclear arms control parallels
- **Ethan Mollick** [argued every AI debate reduces](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-737947c2313b) to the shape of the capability S-curve, offering an influential framing for policy and business discussions
- A **Google** researcher's paper [claiming AI consciousness](/?date=2026-04-28&category=social#item-07b8c2ee7f5d) is mathematically impossible sparked philosophical debate across the community

themes.name: OpenAI-Microsoft Partnership Restructuring
themes.description: Sam Altman announced a major update to the OpenAI-Microsoft partnership, enabling multi-cloud distribution while maintaining Microsoft as primary partner. Gary Marcus frames this as a predicted 'divorce.' Revenue share through 2030, model provision through 2032.

themes.name: AI-Native Team Transformation
themes.description: Andrew Ng's detailed essay on how AI-native software teams operate: engineers becoming generalists, 1:1 engineer-to-PM ratios, small co-located teams, and downstream bottlenecks in marketing/legal/design as coding accelerates.

themes.name: US-China AI Geopolitics / Manus Acquisition
themes.description: China blocked Meta's $2B acquisition of Manus AI, signaling Beijing's hardening stance on AI talent and technology transfer ahead of Trump-Xi meeting. Multiple sources corroborate.

themes.name: Multi-Agent AI Systems Research
themes.description: hardmaru presents ICLR 2026 paper on a 'Conductor' model trained with RL to manage pools of LLMs, dynamically adapting strategies. Introduces 'chain-of-command' paradigm. Powers Sakana's Fugu system.

themes.name: Vibe Coding Disasters & AI Agent Safety
themes.description: Gary Marcus leads an extensive thread arguing AI agents and vibe coding are premature, citing growing Reddit reports of data loss. Key arguments: system prompts/guardrails don't reliably constrain agents, inexperienced users are most at risk, skilled engineers remain essential. Directly counters Dario Amodei's predictions.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Reception and OpenAI
themes.description: Sam Altman celebrates positive developer reception to GPT-5.5 (released April 23), Musk lawsuit implications, and broader OpenAI ecosystem dynamics.

themes.name: DeepSeek V4 Ecosystem
themes.description: vLLM adding day-0 support for DeepSeek V4 base models with technical details on fp4/fp8 quantization. DeepSeek V4 featured in daily AI summaries as a major release.

themes.name: AI Safety, Governance, and International Treaties
themes.description: Yudkowsky's detailed proposals for international AI treaties, GPU export controls, nuclear arms control analogies, and the debate between building dangerous AI vs. warning about it.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Early Demonstrations
themes.description: Multiple posts showcasing GPT-5.5 capabilities: Greg Brockman demonstrates GPU kernel writing, Ethan Mollick shows it creating a tabletop RPG system via Codex. Released just days prior (April 23).

themes.name: AI S-Curve & Capability Assessment
themes.description: Ethan Mollick argues all AI debates reduce to two questions about the S-curve: how good and how fast. He also describes the 'jagged frontier' — AI's uneven capabilities requiring human help at many points.

items.title: AI-native software engineering teams operate very differently than traditional teams. The obvious di...
items.content:
AI-native software engineering teams operate very differently than traditional teams. The obvious difference is that AI-native teams use coding agents to build products much faster, but this leads to many other changes in how we operate. For example, some great engineers now play broader roles than just writing code. They are partly product managers, designers, sometimes marketers. Further, small teams who work in the same office, where they can communicate face-to-face, can move incredibly quickly.

Because we can now build fast, a greater fraction of time must be spent deciding what to build. To deal with this project-management bottleneck, some teams are pushing engineer:product manager (PM) some teams are pushing engineer:product manager (PM) ratios downward from, say, 8:1 to as low as 1:1. But we can do even better: If we have one PM who decides what to build and one engineer who builds it, the communication between them becomes a bottleneck. This is why the fastest-moving teams I see tend to have engineers who know how to do some product work (and, optionally, some PMs who know how to do some engineering work). When an engineer understands users and can make decisions on what to build and build it directly, they can execute incredibly quickly.

I’ve seen engineers successfully expand their roles to including making product decisions, and PMs expand their roles to building software. The tech industry has more engineers than PMs, but both are promising paths. If you are an engineer, you’ll find it useful to learn some product management skills, and if you’re a PM, please learn to build!

Looking beyond the product-management bottleneck, I also see bottlenecks in design, marketing, legal compliance, and much more. When we speed up coding 10x or 100x, everything else becomes slow in comparison. For example, some of my teams have built great features so quickly that the marketing organization was left scrambling to figure out how to communicate them to users — a marketing bottleneck. Or when a team can build software in a day that the legal department needs a week to review, that’s a legal compliance bottleneck. In this way, agentic coding isn’t just changing the workflow of software engineering, it’s also changing all the teams around it.

When smaller, AI-enabled teams can get more done, generalists excel. Traditional companies need to pull together people from many specialties — engineering, product management, design, marketing, legal, etc. — to execute projects and create value. This has resulted in large teams of specialists who work together. But if a team of 2 persons is to get work done that require 5 different specialities, then some of those individuals must play roles outside a single speciality. In some small teams, individuals do have deep specializations. For example, one might be a great engineer and another a great PM. But they also understand the other key functions needed to move a project forward, and can jump into thinking through other kinds of problems as needed. Of course, proficiency with AI tools is a big help, since it helps us to think through problems that involve different roles.

Even in a two-person team, to move fast, communication bottlenecks also must be minimized. This is why I value teams that work in the same location. Remote teams can perform well too, but the highest speed is achieved by having everyone in the room, able to communicate instantaneously to solve problems.

This post focuses on AI-native teams with around 2-10 persons, but not everything can be done by a small team. I'll address the coordination of larger teams in the future.

I realize these shifts to job roles are tough to navigate for many people. At the same time, I am encouraged that individuals and small teams who are willing to learn the relevant skills are now able to get far more done than was possible before. This is the golden age of learning and building!

[Original text: https://t.co/1pUxNC5UXk ]

items.title:
China just blocked Meta's $ 2B December acquisition of Manus and told Zuck to unwind the deal.

Manu...
items.content:
China just blocked Meta's $ 2B December acquisition of Manus and told Zuck to unwind the deal.

Manus is technically Singapore-incorporated, but its founders, parent company, and offices in Beijing and Wuhan are all Chinese.

Awkwardly, the teams are already merged, with Manus engineers reportedly working at Meta's Singapore office for months.

Several of its execs have also been restricted from leaving China.

Just weeks before Trump and Xi sit down, Beijing just drew a major line for Chinese AI talent.

items.title: For the past few years, humans have been doing “prompt engineering” to coax the best performance out...
items.content:
For the past few years, humans have been doing “prompt engineering” to coax the best performance out of different LLMs. In this work, we explored what happens if we train an AI to do that job instead.

By training a Conductor model with RL, we found that it naturally learns to write highly effective, custom instructions for a whole pool of other models. It essentially learns to ‘manage’ them in natural language.

What surprised me most was how it dynamically adapts. For simple factual questions, it just queries one model. But for hard coding problems, it autonomously spins up a whole pipeline of planners, coders, and verifiers.

Really excited to see where this paradigm of “AI managing AI” goes next, especially as we start moving from single-agent chain-of-thought to multi-agent “chain-of-command”.

Link to our #ICLR2026 paper: https://t.co/EwbjjRPLUb

Along with our TRINITY paper which we announced earlier, this work also powers our new multi-agent system: Sakana Fugu (https://t.co/2m8VRdOYqG) 🐡
items.author: @hardmaru
items.summary: As first reported in [Social](/?date=2026-04-27&category=social#item-cbf7d146a7e1) yesterday, hardmaru presents an ICLR 2026 paper on training a 'Conductor' model with RL to manage a pool of LLMs — learning to write custom instructions, dynamically adapting strategies (single model for simple tasks, multi-agent pipelines for complex ones). Connected to Sakana's Fugu system.
items.reasoning: Original research announcement from a well-known researcher (head of Sakana AI). ICLR 2026 paper on AI-managing-AI paradigm. Very high engagement (103K views). Novel concept of 'chain-of-command' vs chain-of-thought. Introduces concrete product (Sakana Fugu).

items.title:
vLLM support for DeepSeek V4 base models is on the way!

The V4 release includes 4 models: base/inst...
items.content:
vLLM support for DeepSeek V4 base models is on the way!

The V4 release includes 4 models: base/instruct × flash/pro. Initial support covers the instruct versions. To extend support to the base models, we worked with @deepseek_ai to add an expert_dtype field in the config, making it easy to distinguish between them (fp4 for instruct, fp8 for base).

Config commit: https://t.co/EZRpjQhwES
vLLM PR: https://t.co/ioAZ1acnou

Model support isn't easy, but close collaboration with model vendors keeps making it better. That trust is what makes vLLM the default home for day-0 support of impactful models.
#vLLM #DeepSeek #LLM #AIInfrastructure #OpenWeights #OpenSourceAI
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-04-26&category=news#item-c4f92ddc9727) coverage of [DeepSeek V4](/?date=2026-04-26&category=news#item-c4f92ddc9727), vLLM announces support for DeepSeek V4 base models is coming. V4 includes 4 models (base/instruct × flash/pro). They collaborated with DeepSeek to add expert_dtype field to distinguish fp4 (instruct) vs fp8 (base) models.
items.reasoning: Important technical infrastructure update. DeepSeek V4 just launched (GA: April 24), and vLLM day-0 support is critical for the open-source ecosystem. Specific technical details about fp4 vs fp8 quantization are valuable. Good engagement (125 likes, 7.4k views).

vLLM support for DeepSeek V4 base models is on the way!

The V4 release includes 4 models: base/instruct × flash/pro. Initial support covers the instruct versions. To extend support to the base models, we worked with @deepseek_ai to add an expert_dtype field in the config, making it easy to distinguish between them (fp4 for instruct, fp8 for base).

Config commit: https://t.co/EZRpjQhwES

vLLM PR: https://t.co/ioAZ1acnou

Model support isn't easy, but close collaboration with model vendors keeps making it better. That trust is what makes vLLM the default home for day-0 support of impactful models.

#vLLM #DeepSeek #LLM #AIInfrastructure #OpenWeights #OpenSourceAI

items.title: @SecScottBessent Unless we unwarrantedly reject concerns dating back to the 1920s and hundreds of mo...
items.content:
@SecScottBessent Unless we unwarrantedly reject concerns dating back to the 1920s and hundreds of modern expert statements that AI extinction risk is a concern, the world needs to step back.  The USA should not try to halt alone; that wouldn't work.  So Senator Sanders seems to me to be straightforwardly taking the correct posture here -- unless we unwarrantedly and without any reasonable argument reject the possibility of superhuman AI; or even more bizarrely, suppose that it will serve humanity despite the lack of any control proposals that stand up to five minutes of informed criticism.

We should be imposing strict export controls on GPUs and adding hardware controls to them, contrary to Nvidia's lobbying and sacred bottom line; and even more so restrict the ASML equipment required to make the most advanced chips.  But without a treaty that can only contain China for a few years.

This is humanity's problem.  In the old days there were international treaties, including among enemies, meant to prevent not just nuclear war but the buildup to an actualized nuclear war.  International coordination is required to prevent the buildup to uncontrolled machine superintelligence, including the uncontrolled proliferation of capabilities that would then inevitably lead to machine superintelligence being built somewhere.

The decision to never talk to the Soviets about nuclear war would have only made sense if you believed there was otherwise zero chance of nuclear war.  We do not think this is a defensible stance to hold about superhuman AI or loss of control of machine superintelligence.
items.author: @ESYudkowsky
items.summary: Yudkowsky makes a detailed case for international AI governance treaties, comparing the situation to nuclear arms control. Argues for GPU export controls, ASML equipment restrictions, and coordination with China to prevent uncontrolled machine superintelligence.
items.reasoning: Substantive, detailed policy proposal from the most prominent AI safety advocate. Engages with Senator Sanders' position. 136 likes. One of the most policy-concrete posts in this batch. References real geopolitical levers (ASML, GPU exports).
items.summary_html: Yudkowsky makes a detailed case for international AI governance treaties, comparing the situation to nuclear arms control. Argues for GPU export controls, ASML equipment restrictions, and coordination with China to prevent uncontrolled machine superintelligence.

items.title:
A Google researcher just proved AI consciousness is mathematically impossible. 

Not in 10 years. No...
items.content:
A Google researcher just proved AI consciousness is mathematically impossible. 

Not in 10 years. Not in 100. Ever.

The argument is structural, not technical. 

Computation is a description of a process, not the process itself.

For something to "compute," a conscious observer must first carve reality into symbols and assign meaning. 

Without that observer, there are only voltage gradients.

The paper calls this the Abstraction Fallacy.

The analogy that makes it click:

> A GPU can simulate photosynthesis perfectly
> It will never produce glucose
> Simulation is not instantiation
> Maps don't become territory

The framework doesn't rule out artificial sentience entirely. 

It says if a machine were ever aware, it would be from its physical makeup, not its code.

Scaling parameters cannot change category.

items.title: Every AI discussion ultimately rests on two questions: how good can AI get? And how fast? They are p...
items.content:
Every AI discussion ultimately rests on two questions: how good can AI get? And how fast? They are predictions about the s-curve shape.

Everything else (job impact, potential risks, etc.) is downstream of those questions. I think it would be useful to focus on them more often. https://t.co/bmlrW8cfHk
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick argues every AI discussion reduces to two questions: how good can AI get, and how fast (the S-curve shape). Job impact, risks, etc. are all downstream of these.
items.reasoning: Highly influential framing from a top AI commentator. Very high engagement (27K views, 450 likes). Provides a unifying analytical framework for AI debates. Original insight that clarifies discourse.

items.title: ⚠️ AI agents are wildly premature technology that is being rolled out way too fast. The deepest less...
items.content:
⚠️ AI agents are wildly premature technology that is being rolled out way too fast. The deepest lesson about the vibe coded AI agent disaster story that is running around is NOT about losing your data. ⚠️

𝗜𝘁’𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗔𝗜 𝘀𝗮𝗳𝗲𝘁𝘆. 

The user wasn’t (totally) naive. He thought system prompts and guardrails would save him. They didn’t. 

In this case he lost data. Eventually people will lose lives.

items.title:
China is blocking the Manus acquisition by Meta. A few things stand out:

- China has historically b...
items.content:
China is blocking the Manus acquisition by Meta. A few things stand out:

- China has historically been more opaque and less public in restricting foreign acquisitions of Chinese tech assets, while the US (CFIUS) has been more explicit and aggressive (e.g. Lattice)
- Manus had moved to Singapore, suggesting China may prioritize origin of technology and talent over legal domicile
- Historically these reviews focused more on hardware, telecom, and core IP. This signals AI software, data, and distribution layers are now clearly in scope
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima analyzes China blocking Meta's acquisition of Manus (AI agent company), noting China is now treating AI software/data as strategic assets subject to foreign acquisition review, even when companies relocate (Manus moved to Singapore).
items.reasoning: Substantive geopolitical analysis of a significant event - China blocking Meta's Manus acquisition. Original insights about AI software now being in scope for national security reviews and legal domicile vs. technology origin. From credible AI builder/investor.
items.summary_html: Yohei Nakajima analyzes China blocking Meta's acquisition of Manus (AI agent company), noting China is now treating AI software/data as strategic assets subject to foreign acquisition review, even when companies relocate (Manus moved to Singapore).
items.content_html:
China is blocking the Manus acquisition by Meta. A few things stand out:

  • China has historically been more opaque and less public in restricting foreign acquisitions of Chinese tech assets, while the US (CFIUS) has been more explicit and aggressive (e.g. Lattice)
  • Manus had moved to Singapore, suggesting China may prioritize origin of technology and talent over legal domicile
  • Historically these reviews focused more on hardware, telecom, and core IP. This signals AI software, data, and distribution layers are now clearly in scope

items.title:
The only way to fully appreciate the jaggedness of the AI frontier is up close.

When you use it for...
items.content:
The only way to fully appreciate the jaggedness of the AI frontier is up close.

When you use it for a task you know well you find tons of tiny points where AI requires human help. Some are tedious (move a thing) & some profound (is this idea good)? But there are many, for now.

items.title:
A deep dive from @awscloud × @RedHat_AI into FP8 KV-cache + attention in vLLM. 
The headline fix: tw...
items.content:
A deep dive from @awscloud × @RedHat_AI into FP8 KV-cache + attention in vLLM. 
The headline fix: two-level accumulation in FA3 takes 128k needle-in-a-haystack from 13% → 89%, while keeping the FP8 decode speedup. Plus a new --kv-cache-dtype-skip-layers flag for hybrid-attention models like gpt-oss.
📝 https://t.co/DEpDanVewb
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM shares a deep dive from AWS and Red Hat on FP8 KV-cache and attention optimizations, highlighting a fix where two-level accumulation in FlashAttention 3 improves needle-in-a-haystack from 13% to 89%, plus a new hybrid-attention flag for models like GPT-OSS.
items.reasoning: 103 likes from technical audience. Highly technical and impactful: a 13% → 89% improvement in needle-in-a-haystack is a massive accuracy fix for FP8 inference. AWS + Red Hat collaboration on vLLM. Mentions GPT-OSS specifically.
items.summary_html: vLLM shares a deep dive from AWS and Red Hat on FP8 KV-cache and attention optimizations, highlighting a fix where two-level accumulation in FlashAttention 3 improves needle-in-a-haystack from 13% to 89%, plus a new hybrid-attention flag for models like GPT-OSS.
items.content_html:
A deep dive from @awscloud × @RedHat_AI into FP8 KV-cache + attention in vLLM.

The headline fix: two-level accumulation in FA3 takes 128k needle-in-a-haystack from 13% → 89%, while keeping the FP8 decode speedup. Plus a new --kv-cache-dtype-skip-layers flag for hybrid-attention models like gpt-oss.

📝 https://t.co/DEpDanVewb

items.title: People keep sending me this clip of @iamjohnoliver using my tweet as evidence that AI models don’t w...
items.content:
People keep sending me this clip of @iamjohnoliver using my tweet as evidence that AI models don’t work well.

Just to clear up any confusion, with respect, the tweet was a) taken way out of context and b) extremely outdated.

The model in question (4o) is multiple generations old, and was shut down for being too sycophantic. Current models would not have behaved this way.

It’s sort of like looking at a Nokia flip phone and saying “this isn’t useful”, when an iPhone exists.

John, I’m a fan, and welcome any discussion here. Just want things to be accurate and not misleading!
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer responds to John Oliver using his old tweet (about GPT-4o sycophancy) as evidence AI doesn't work. Clarifies the tweet was taken out of context, the model (4o) is generations old and was shut down for being too sycophantic. Current models would not behave this way.
items.reasoning: High engagement (474 likes, 36k views). Interesting case of AI discourse reaching mainstream media (John Oliver) and the challenges of outdated examples being used. The correction about model generations is factually important.

items.title: People keep blaming the users for the growing number of vibe-coded reasoners. Doing so is *half* rig...
items.content:
People keep blaming the users for the growing number of vibe-coded reasoners. Doing so is *half* right.

Users *are*screwing up - by letting vibe coded stuff access their files, without proper backups, without proper monitoring, without adequate sysadmin, etc.

But that’s *exactly why* we still need software engineers — and why what Dario recently said about software engineers disappearing is so absurd.

Skilled coders can use vibe-coded tools, with careful oversight.

But amateurs using vibe-coding tools are asking for trouble. 

Using them without a strong prior working knowledge in coding and sysadmin *is* the mistake.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues vibe coders share blame but the deeper issue is that skilled engineers are still needed — directly countering Dario Amodei's claim that software engineers will disappear. Amateurs using vibe coding without coding/sysadmin knowledge is the real mistake.
items.reasoning: Substantive rebuttal to Dario Amodei's statements about SE disappearing. Good engagement (22K views). Nuanced position: users share blame but the hype around vibe coding is dangerous. Connects to broader future-of-work debate.
items.summary_html: Marcus argues vibe coders share blame but the deeper issue is that skilled engineers are still needed — directly countering Dario Amodei's claim that software engineers will disappear. Amateurs using vibe coding without coding/sysadmin knowledge is the real mistake.
items.content_html:
People keep blaming the users for the growing number of vibe-coded reasoners. Doing so is *half* right.

Users *are*screwing up - by letting vibe coded stuff access their files, without proper backups, without proper monitoring, without adequate sysadmin, etc.

But that’s *exactly why* we still need software engineers — and why what Dario recently said about software engineers disappearing is so absurd.

Skilled coders can use vibe-coded tools, with careful oversight.

But amateurs using vibe-coding tools are asking for trouble.

Using them without a strong prior working knowledge in coding and sysadmin *is* the mistake.

items.title:
A folder of 21 markdown files just hit #1 on GitHub trending.

mattpocock/skills crossed 28,000+ sta...
items.content:
A folder of 21 markdown files just hit #1 on GitHub trending.

mattpocock/skills crossed 28,000+ stars on April 27, up from 18,700 two days earlier.

No SDK. No framework. No application code. Each skill is a SKILL.md file under 200 lines, with YAML frontmatter that tells the agent when to use it.

Five skills compose the daily chain:

> /grill-me : interrogates the user on every branch of a design before any code is written
> /to-prd : synthesizes the current conversation into a PRD and files it as a GitHub issue
> /to-issues : splits the PRD into vertical-slice tracer-bullet issues
> /tdd : enforces red-green-refactor one slice at a time, banning bulk-written tests
> /improve-codebase-architecture : finds shallow modules to deepen, with a fixed 8-word vocabulary

Install one at a time:
npx skills@latest add mattpocock/skills/<name>

Works on Claude Code and similar agents. MIT licensed.

The structural surprise is /grill-me itself.

11 lines including frontmatter. The YAML description is longer than any paragraph in the body. The skill walks the design tree, asks one question at a time, recommends an answer, and explores the codebase when a question can be answered from code.

A live wrinkle: Claude Code's Auto Mode silently injects system-prompt instructions that override skills like /grill-me. Run with Auto Mode off until the override behavior settles.

Taste and narrow scope just outpaced framework reach.

Full breakdown below.

items.title:
"Summarize from here" in Claude Code is awesome:

Here is how it works:

1. Hit Esc+Esc or type /rew...
items.content:
"Summarize from here" in Claude Code is awesome:

Here is how it works:

1. Hit Esc+Esc or type /rewind. This will open the checkpoint menu with a scrollable list of all checkpoints Claude has created during the session.

2. Pick the checkpoint you want to summarize from. Everything before this checkpoint stays intact, and everything after it will get compressed.

3. When you select the checkpoint, you'll see the "Summarize from here" option. This is the one you need to select.

Claude will collapse all messages from that checkpoint forward into a compact summary. The conversation history before the checkpoint will stay exactly as it was.

This is what you want to do after a long session where you worked on specs, architectural decisions, and constraints, but Claude went on a debugging detour and started installing a library, or did something else that added a lot of garbage to the context.

If you run /compact, you'll be summarizing the entire context, and you don't want that.

Instead, do the "summarize from here" trick to keep everything valuable in place.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago provides a detailed tutorial on Claude Code's 'Summarize from here' feature: using Esc+Esc or /rewind to access checkpoints, then selectively compressing conversation history from a specific point rather than the entire context (as /compact does). Useful for preserving valuable early context while removing debugging detours.

"Summarize from here" in Claude Code is awesome:

Here is how it works:

1. Hit Esc+Esc or type /rewind. This will open the checkpoint menu with a scrollable list of all checkpoints Claude has created during the session.

2. Pick the checkpoint you want to summarize from. Everything before this checkpoint stays intact, and everything after it will get compressed.

3. When you select the checkpoint, you'll see the "Summarize from here" option. This is the one you need to select.

Claude will collapse all messages from that checkpoint forward into a compact summary. The conversation history before the checkpoint will stay exactly as it was.

This is what you want to do after a long session where you worked on specs, architectural decisions, and constraints, but Claude went on a debugging detour and started installing a library, or did something else that added a lot of garbage to the context.

If you run /compact, you'll be summarizing the entire context, and you don't want that.

Instead, do the "summarize from here" trick to keep everything valuable in place.

items.title: No matter how hard you, as a closed private company, try to build the strongest AI model, and no mat...
items.content:
No matter how hard you, as a closed private company, try to build the strongest AI model, and no matter how much you spend on achieving your goal, public research scientists will discover your science virtually weeks after your technology is seen.

And given that you don't have any vendor lock-in, clients will go where it's cheaper. And given that your competitors will get the same tech for free from public research, it will be cheaper at your competitors.

Just look at the robotic dogs. Boston Dynamics invested hundreds of millions into their robotic dog. Now, Chinese companies sell them for the price of a scooter because they just saw how it had to be done. And this is robotics, where you can file and enforce a patent.

items.reasoning: Substantive argument about AI business moats from a known ML author. The Boston Dynamics analogy is compelling. Moderate engagement. Touches on open vs. closed source economics.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues closed AI companies can't maintain competitive advantages because public researchers reverse-engineer their science within weeks, and without vendor lock-in, clients go to cheaper alternatives. Uses Boston Dynamics' robot dog vs. Chinese clones as analogy.
items.content_html:
No matter how hard you, as a closed private company, try to build the strongest AI model, and no matter how much you spend on achieving your goal, public research scientists will discover your science virtually weeks after your technology is seen.

And given that you don't have any vendor lock-in, clients will go where it's cheaper. And given that your competitors will get the same tech for free from public research, it will be cheaper at your competitors.

Just look at the robotic dogs. Boston Dynamics invested hundreds of millions into their robotic dog. Now, Chinese companies sell them for the price of a scooter because they just saw how it had to be done. And this is robotics, where you can file and enforce a patent.

items.title: Taylor Swift just filed three federal trademarks to lock her voice and likeness against AI deepfakes...
items.content:
Taylor Swift just filed three federal trademarks to lock her voice and likeness against AI deepfakes.

Specifically: "Hey, it's Taylor," "Hey, it's Taylor Swift," and a stage photo from the Eras Tour.

Matthew McConaughey did the same in December with his own likeness and his "alright, alright, alright" phrase.

Federal trademark filings give them both grounds to sue AI platforms for clips "confusingly similar" to the registered ones.

items.title:
This points to a broader issue where AI is often being judged based on extremely outdated models.

A...
items.content:
This points to a broader issue where AI is often being judged based on extremely outdated models.

Any time someone sends me a study showing AI failing at something, the first thing I do is look at the models they used… more often than not, they’re extremely outdated.

We need to be judging based on the best available stuff, people! Otherwise we’re misleading ourselves about what these AIs are capable of.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer argues AI is often judged based on extremely outdated models. Studies showing AI failures often use old models rather than current best-available ones, which misleads about actual capabilities.
items.reasoning: Important meta-point about AI evaluation methodology. The rapid pace of model improvement means benchmarks and studies age quickly. Good engagement (72 likes, 9k views) and relevant to ongoing capability debates.

items.content:
Once you free your mind you realize that if OpenAI opened up that everyone would adopt it.

Apple is holding lots of things in AI back.

Who will go completely open first? We got leaks that Apple is letting us use any model. WWDC this year will be watched closely because of the new Siri.

@sama bought @openclaw for a reason. To put it everywhere. 

@steipete please keep the faith.

I see how you will put OpenClaw into everyone’s hands.

I told the Siri team the same when that launched in my house. Ask them and they will tell you I got it before Steve Jobs did.

The thing is if Qualcomm is involved they are working on glasses with you too.

There being open will be a real differentiator.

Keep fighting. You are right.

“Always keep a separate backup.” For sure, every experienced programmer should know this.

𝘉𝘶𝘵 𝘸𝘦 𝘫𝘶...
items.content:
“Always keep a separate backup.” For sure, every experienced programmer should know this.

𝘉𝘶𝘵 𝘸𝘦 𝘫𝘶𝘴𝘵 𝘤𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘢 𝘸𝘩𝘰𝘭𝘦 𝘨𝘦𝘯𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘰𝘧 𝘷𝘪𝘣𝘦 𝘤𝘰𝘥𝘦𝘳𝘴 𝘸𝘩𝘰 𝘥𝘰𝘯’𝘵.

And we now have a legion of synthetic coding agents (like Cursor) that also apparently don’t always follow this basic principle, either.

𝗪𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝘀 *𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆* 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝘄𝗲 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀.

items.title:
The case against an acquisition of a proposed merger of Tesla and SpaceX/xAI.

For a Tesla investor:...
items.content:
The case against an acquisition of a proposed merger of Tesla and SpaceX/xAI.

For a Tesla investor: you will suffer for next seven quarters and sell out right when all Elon’s plans are working out. And scale of everything done by Tesla goes exponential. Right about then Optimus will start making itself at scale.

I am expecting SpaceX to be coming too with a huge datacenter win (a string of datacenters to Mars, which means earth is covered)  so not worried. Both have exponential runs ahead in my mind.

For SpaceX investors. You are taking in incredible risk. Tesla will be in debt in seven quarters.

Elon has my vote anyway. 

These two together will be an example of where an acquisition works out. One of the former AOL execs told me years ago their acquisitions made them billions.

I have faith.

But my money manager never sees the world the way I do. He always shows me the risks. And my friend Jeff was first CTO on Wall Street is worried for us investors.

My argument is any dilution is still worth it. Within reason.

The rocket is our future.

And so is the robot.

Elon has to convince all shareholders this is a good idea.

I write this while my eight year old robot drives me home.

We drove to the gold country. And back. Hours of smooth driving. Mostly.

Just arrived back home safely.

Looking at phones cause tragedy.

Robot takes over whenever you want.

Don’t you want to get to the launch pad safely?

They belong together. But there are risks.

Elon could go crazy. 

Oh, wait, he has always been crazy. You think it is sane to take on the auto industry? And a handful of others?

Elon is my kind of crazy.

He broke three laws when he gave me the first ride in first Tesla. We definitely were racing @jason. Street racing is illegal in California.

That is why I love him. 

He takes risks no one else was willing to take.

I will do whatever he tells me to. 

He has earned it over and over and even if screws us we will win. Together. The pie is so large. 

Also Elon has always supported me even when I had a bad reputation. And I still do.

So he could whip me with a cable and I would still vote to merge, if that opportunity ever arises.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble's major thread arguing for/against Tesla-SpaceX merger. Details the bull case (exponential growth, Optimus at scale, SpaceX datacenter constellation to Mars), bear case (Tesla debt in 7 quarters, dilution risk), and shares his autonomous driving experience. Deeply personal advocacy for Elon Musk.

items.title: "By then, you’re lost in what AI psychosis survivors call a “spiral”: a personalized delusion perfec...
items.content: "By then, you’re lost in what AI psychosis survivors call a “spiral”: a personalized delusion perfectly tailored to your psyche, often spending several hours per day talking with the AI. As the AI echo chamber deepens, you become more and more lost. A spiral can range from believing you are channeling interdimensional spirits, to believing you have been chosen by or are in love with a sentient AI, to believing you are now “awakened” or enlightened, like Neo from the Matrix."
items.author: @timnitGebru@dair-community.social

items.title:
Agents that continuously rewrite their own harness just became real. 

Agent infrastructure today sh...
items.content:
Agents that continuously rewrite their own harness just became real. 

Agent infrastructure today ships static agents.

The harness, model, and workflow never change after deployment.

Over time performance degrades silently.

Context drifts, latency compounds, newer models sit unused.

Motus is an open-source project that fixes this by learning in production.

It captures every trace from failures to cost, then continuously tunes the harness, model routing, memory, and latency.

Results speak for themselves:

> Beats any frontier model solo
> 2.3x cheaper across benchmarks
> 52% faster responses
> 45% better recall

Works with any SDK and deploys with a single command under Apache 2.0.

The most underrated piece is memory.

Standard setups dump full history into context until the model chokes or hallucinates.

This one auto-summarizes when token budgets run thin and restores sessions cleanly.

Static agents built once and prayed over are finally done.

Auto-summarizes context when token budgets run low.

items.title: I was on a cruise ship last week (Star of the Seas), and they had pods of 10 elevators in a circle, ...
items.content:
I was on a cruise ship last week (Star of the Seas), and they had pods of 10 elevators in a circle, where you picked your destination floor on a pad, and it directed you to the correct elevator, which was often behind you.

It seemed to work efficiently, but multiple times I saw people tap their floor and just look away, conditioned for normal elevator operation, and miss the arrival of the elevator they were supposed to get on.

Addressing my normal pet peeve of interaction feedback latency would have helped — with all the fades and slides, it takes over a second for the first hint of the elevator to show up, and two seconds for it to fully stabilize. That may not seem like much in some circumstances, but it is plenty of time for people to look away. The elevator letter should appear instantaneously, maybe with some festive animation around it to hold attention that was on the button press.

Even better would be to add a localized audio cue from the elevator the instant you pressed the button, which would let you immediately know where it is without having to scan for the lighted letter.

(the Starlink internet on the ship was excellent, allowing me to get some work in at sea)

items.summary: John Carmack describes his experience with a destination-dispatch elevator system on Star of the Seas cruise ship, critiquing the UI feedback latency (1-2 second delays for elevator assignment display). Proposes instant display with surrounding animations and localized audio cues. Also notes Starlink internet on the ship was excellent.

Proposes instant display with surrounding animations and localized audio cues. Also notes Starlink internet on the ship was excellent.
items.content_html:
I was on a cruise ship last week (Star of the Seas), and they had pods of 10 elevators in a circle, where you picked your destination floor on a pad, and it directed you to the correct elevator, which was often behind you.

It seemed to work efficiently, but multiple times I saw people tap their floor and just look away, conditioned for normal elevator operation, and miss the arrival of the elevator they were supposed to get on.

Addressing my normal pet peeve of interaction feedback latency would have helped — with all the fades and slides, it takes over a second for the first hint of the elevator to show up, and two seconds for it to fully stabilize. That may not seem like much in some circumstances, but it is plenty of time for people to look away. The elevator letter should appear instantaneously, maybe with some festive animation around it to hold attention that was on the button press.

Even better would be to add a localized audio cue from the elevator the instant you pressed the button, which would let you immediately know where it is without having to scan for the lighted letter.

(the Starlink internet on the ship was excellent, allowing me to get some work in at sea)

items.title: Processing loan applications traditionally takes a staggering amount of time going through paperwork...
items.content:
Processing loan applications traditionally takes a staggering amount of time going through paperwork - ~dozens of hours every month looking at the loan application and cross-checking it with user-submitted data: tax returns, bank statements, pay stubs, and more. 

A big reason it takes so much time is that you need to check numbers (e.g. income) are consistent between different documents. 

You can automate this with AI agents, but this requires that you have extremely high accuracy document OCR that can properly extract the right information out of each document.

I wrote this blog post to show you how you can build an agentic workflow to automate the e2e process. It uses LlamaParse for high-accuracy document OCR, and integrates with Claude to give back structured outputs.

Blog: https://t.co/Hn7TiHJH9p

Full repo is here: https://t.co/JZ2JsNx784
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) sharing a blog post about using AI agents to automate loan application processing with LlamaParse for OCR and Claude for structured outputs.
items.reasoning: Practical AI use case from the LlamaIndex founder. Document processing in financial services is a major enterprise AI application. Includes full repo. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) sharing a blog post about using AI agents to automate loan application processing with LlamaParse for OCR and Claude for structured outputs.
items.content_html:
Processing loan applications traditionally takes a staggering amount of time going through paperwork - ~dozens of hours every month looking at the loan application and cross-checking it with user-submitted data: tax returns, bank statements, pay stubs, and more.

A big reason it takes so much time is that you need to check numbers (e.g. income) are consistent between different documents.

You can automate this with AI agents, but this requires that you have extremely high accuracy document OCR that can properly extract the right information out of each document.

I wrote this blog post to show you how you can build an agentic workflow to automate the e2e process. It uses LlamaParse for high-accuracy document OCR, and integrates with Claude to give back structured outputs.

Blog: https://t.co/Hn7TiHJH9p

Full repo is here: https://t.co/JZ2JsNx784

items.title: This is the best analogy for AI-generated content, including AI-generated code. Yes, it's ugly. Yes,...
items.content:
This is the best analogy for AI-generated content, including AI-generated code. Yes, it's ugly. Yes, it's made of stolen pieces of other people's content. Yes, you cannot fix it manually. But it does the job, and it costs virtually nothing to build and fix compared to using human developers.

This doesn't mean that human developers shouldn't write and debug code by hand. Just not this kind of code.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov provides an analogy for AI-generated content: it's ugly, made from others' work, can't be manually fixed, but it's cheap and functional. Argues human developers should still write code by hand — just not this kind of code.
items.reasoning: Moderate engagement. Articulates a nuanced position on AI-generated code that acknowledges both its value and limitations.
items.summary_html: Burkov provides an analogy for AI-generated content: it's ugly, made from others' work, can't be manually fixed, but it's cheap and functional. Argues human developers should still write code by hand — just not this kind of code.
items.content_html:
This is the best analogy for AI-generated content, including AI-generated code. Yes, it's ugly. Yes, it's made of stolen pieces of other people's content. Yes, you cannot fix it manually. But it does the job, and it costs virtually nothing to build and fix compared to using human developers.

This doesn't mean that human developers shouldn't write and debug code by hand. Just not this kind of code.

She is the inaugural winner of the Vera Rubin New Frontiers Prize, presented by our CEO Jensen Huang and @yurimilner at the 12th @brkthroughprize ceremony.

items.title:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but beca...
items.content:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon's NVIDIA-partnered thread argues enterprise AI strategy must shift from the model layer to reasoning systems with tools, context retention, and secure execution environments.
items.reasoning: Thread opener with substantive strategic framing about the enterprise AI shift from model-centric to system-centric thinking. NVIDIA partnership adds credibility despite sponsored nature.
items.summary_html: Van Loon's NVIDIA-partnered thread argues enterprise AI strategy must shift from the model layer to reasoning systems with tools, context retention, and secure execution environments.
items.content_html:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC

My friend and I had my robot drive us from San Jo...
items.content:
These pictures and video thanks to AI from Tesla.

My friend and I had my robot drive us from San Jose to Angel Camp.

It wasn’t perfect. It is an eight year old robot working with old model on a shitty computer.

But it is science fiction to me.

The first autonomous car couldn’t complete a far simpler course.

We are getting close. To saving many lives.

Here's a uv one-liner that downloads and runs the MLX model against a local mp3 file

uv run --with ...
items.content:
Here's a uv one-liner that downloads and runs the MLX model against a local mp3 file

uv run --with mlx-audio python -m mlx_audio.stt.generate \
  --model mlx-community/VibeVoice-ASR-4bit \
  --audio lenny.mp3 --output-path lenny \
  --format json --verbose --max-tokens 32768

items.title:
What separates companies that transform in the agentic era from those simply upgrade tech stack?

At...
items.content:
What separates companies that transform in the agentic era from those simply upgrade tech stack?

At Salesforce TDX, I spoke with Raju Malhotra, @CertiniaInc about this shift from systems of record → intelligence → execution.

Having been both a solution architect at Oracle and a Chief Customer Officer, I’ve lived the gap between knowing what to do and being able to act in real time.

Agentic AI is starting to close that gap.

Systems are no longer just advising decisions; they’re beginning to execute them.

Watch the full conversation: 
https://t.co/n7c9O0pImq

Learn more about @Certinia Veda: https://t.co/ItdoK17dH6

#Agentforce #Veda #CertiniaPartner #TDX26

items.title:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system ...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system around them:

→ runtimes
→ memory
→ tool access
→ orchestration
→ secure execution environments

That is what turns a model into an agent, and an agent into something the business can actually deploy across functions.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon argues models are becoming components, and the real value is in the system around them: runtimes, memory, tool access, orchestration, and secure execution.
items.reasoning: Strong insight about the commoditization of models and value migration to the agentic systems layer. Part of NVIDIA partnership thread but the observation is widely validated.
items.summary_html: Van Loon argues models are becoming components, and the real value is in the system around them: runtimes, memory, tool access, orchestration, and secure execution.
items.content_html:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system around them:

→ runtimes

→ memory

→ tool access

→ orchestration

→ secure execution environments

That is what turns a model into an agent, and an agent into something the business can actually deploy across functions.

items.title:
Everyone thinks they’re scaling AI.

Most are just scaling chaos.

What I’m seeing, including in con...
items.content:
Everyone thinks they’re scaling AI.

Most are just scaling chaos.

What I’m seeing, including in conversations with @collibra, is a dangerous illusion

→ faster experimentation
→ more pilots
→ zero control

The gap between AI ambition and actual business value is widening.

Here’s what’s really going on…

items.title:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open model...
items.content:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open models and proprietary models.

They do not.

The smarter path is hybrid.

→ proprietary models for scale and broad capability
→ open models for flexibility and control
→ orchestration layers to route each task to the best-fit model in real time

That is how you balance performance, cost, and governance without locking yourself into one lane.

items.title:
The real bottleneck isn’t models.

It’s unstructured data.

I saw a side-by-side demo that made this...
items.content:
The real bottleneck isn’t models.

It’s unstructured data.

I saw a side-by-side demo that made this obvious:

→ Raw data environment
• lower accuracy
• higher risk
• inefficient outputs

→ Metadata-enriched environment
• better decisions
• faster results
• measurable value

Same AI. Completely different outcomes.

The difference? Governance and structure.

Here’s the bottom line:

AI won’t be won by who moves fastest.
It’ll be won by who governs best.

If your data isn’t structured, contextualized, and controlled, scaling AI will only amplify problems.

I break this down in detail in my latest video with Collibra.

Check out the full article: https://t.co/G6PX8FzxjA

items.title:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they ...
items.content:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they can govern
→ Outputs are inconsistent and hard to trust
→ Risk is increasing quietly in the background

Without a solid data foundation, AI lacks context.

And without context, AI is just… guesswork.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon warns that teams are innovating faster than they can govern, creating increasing hidden risk.
items.reasoning: Part of the Collibra governance thread - reasonable point about governance lagging innovation.
items.summary_html: Van Loon warns that teams are innovating faster than they can govern, creating increasing hidden risk.
items.content_html:
The biggest misconception?

Speed = progress.

In reality:

→ Teams are innovating faster than they can govern

→ Outputs are inconsistent and hard to trust

→ Risk is increasing quietly in the background

Without a solid data foundation, AI lacks context.

And without context, AI is just… guesswork.

items.content:
@DanAdvantage @OpenAI Well, Sam has bought a lot of RAM, so I have a feeling he's pretty good at scaling.

That is the big problem:
1. Can he do it at scale?
2. Can he get people to buy at Apple-like volumes?
3. Can he satisfy that demand?

Even if it is 1/100th, it would be impressive.

No one says that people will stop writing software by hand, but people will stop reading AI-generated code.

items.title: GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorc...
items.content:
GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

The rapid advancement of large model technology is leading to an increasing application of agent technology across various fields and industries significantly transforming how people work and live. In complex and dynamic environments, multi-agent systems are able to tackle intricate tasks that would be challenging for a single agent, thanks to their collaborative and division-of-labor approaches. 

The following stack of research papers and hands on tutorials highlight the integrated use of GPT with  LangGraph and CrewAI. LangGraph enhances information transmission efficiency through its graph-based structure, while CrewAI boosts team collaboration and system performance via intelligent task allocation and resource management. The key areas of this research include: 

The design of agent architectures based on LangGraph for precise control . The enhancement of agent capabilities through CrewAI to tackle a range of tasks. The goal of this study is to explore the combined potential of GPT and LangGraph and CrewAI in multi-agent systems, offering fresh insights for the ongoing evolution of agent technology and fostering innovation in the application of large model intelligent agents.

References 

Duan, Z., & Wang, J. (2024, November 27). Exploration of LLM multi-agent application implementation based on LangGraph+CrewAI. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/pc2HNby2wx

Horsey, J. (2025, March 9). Build a powerful Python chatbot in minutes with LangGraph. Geeky Gadgets. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/xpp0gyYrgg

Ong, R. (2024, July 10). GPT-4o and LangGraph tutorial: Build a TNT-LLM application. DataCamp. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/wzv4BeoSlC

Sivan, V. (2024). Building AI agent systems with LangGraph. Medium. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/oJO20UXX4n

Wang, J., & Duan, Z. (2024, December 2). Intelligent Spark agents: A modular LangGraph framework for scalable, visualized, and enhanced big data machine learning workflows. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/IONuxFVJZZ

items.content:
New Release (2nd edition) from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/hZ56X8ADN7

RAG-Driven Generative AI — Build MAS-RAG with DualRAG, GraphRAG, multimodal video pipelines, and Oracle Database 23ai

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀:
🟢Master DualRAG by combining vector search with SQL filtering over structured enterprise data.

🟢Implement GraphRAG, Spatial-RAG, and vector search natively in Oracle Database 23ai.

🟢Build multimodal video pipelines with human-feedback loops and fine-tuned models.

MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.

items.content:
I'm HIRING 5 more people to help me build the future of AI media.

The Rundown is closing in on 3M active readers, and the demand is far outpacing what we can handle.

$2,000 referral bonus if you help us find someone we hire full-time.

Open roles:

> Head of Content -- lead the editorial team that reaches millions every day
> Head of Sales -- run a scrappy sales team and revenue strategy across our media properties
> Community Lead -- turn our 3M+ readership into the "Reddit for AI use cases"
> Ad Creative Strategist -- research and ship ad creatives that hook the next million AI-curious readers
> Branded Content Editor -- turn brands into stories our readers actually want to read

What we're looking for among all roles:

> Deep AI curiosity (you've already replaced part of your workflow with agents)
> Unreasonably biased toward action
> Taste sharp enough to spot great work (and AI slop)

All roles are fully remote. Link below to apply!

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical compon...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.

Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.

Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.

Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode  

References

Jeon, S., Kim, M., Yoon, J., & et al. (2023). Machine learning-based obesity classification considering 3D body scanner measurements. Scientific Reports, 13, 3299. Retrieved February 27, 2025, from https://t.co/h3ZR1dBTMI

items.summary: Discusses research on using 3D body scanning and machine learning to improve obesity classification beyond BMI, using paired 3D scan and DXA data from Korean population.
items.reasoning: Most substantive gp_pulipaka post with actual research summary, but references a 2023 paper so not timely. Low engagement.
items.summary_html: Discusses research on using 3D body scanning and machine learning to improve obesity classification beyond BMI, using paired 3D scan and DXA data from Korean population.

items.title: Cyber Resilience (CR) in the Agentic Era goes beyond reactive recovery from a data spill or cyber in...
items.content:
Cyber Resilience (CR) in the Agentic Era goes beyond reactive recovery from a data spill or cyber intrusion.

Let's talk about that — deep CR, business continuity, and how @Commvault can meet your toughest enterprise CR requirements.

There are many dimensions to CR, including: AI integration in CR operations, identity resilience (protection & recovery), proactive monitoring & threat scanning (for early detection & warning), clean recovery (from data loss & potential data poisoning), smooth communication across all enterprise players & stakeholders, tools with the end-user in mind, and hybrid (on-prem + cloud) & multi-cloud integration.

Minimizing two most-critical variables (downtime and data loss) simultaneously in a complex enterprise IT environment requires deep understanding of the interrelationship and interactions among many variables, end-points, storage-points, applications, etc. Managing that complexity is not for the faint-hearted.

@Commvault steps up to these challenges. Here are some highlights of their AI-powered services and offerings. Which of these are most relevant and/or exciting to you?
1) The Platform: https://t.co/eEOZb5cC5G
2) Leveraging AI: https://t.co/XoRU44DjxK
3) Cleanroom Recovery: https://t.co/Q7aOtWTPdm
4) Identity Resilience: https://t.co/r93b5hO05f
5) Automated Recovery for Business Continuity: https://t.co/UuO18tm9Xz
6) Unified Resilience Ops: https://t.co/ZPtXOI7u2L
7) Cloud-Native Operations: https://t.co/6ZQ9uvE7uz
8) Use Cases: https://t.co/iZN5srJCNR
9) Supporting Environments and Partnerships: https://t.co/BesHSAMiMZ
10) Learning Programs: https://t.co/4LmmgGAmqu
11) Latest Releases & Announcements: https://t.co/ytmVOpT4uJ

Where would you begin?

If you are not sure, then start here: https://t.co/WTMAQjr8WY

Or Request a Demo: https://t.co/fIbmbPCLAE

#Commvault #CyberResilience #DataResilience #BusinessContinuity #GoogleCloudNext #AgenticAI #DataProtection #ResOps #sponsored

Would you like to smell like you just downed a bottle of gin, like a leather couch, or like you were sitting on the wrong side of a campfire? Women get all the nice fruits and flowers. This stuff shouldn’t be gendered anyway like most things.
